title: Acute Ischemic Stroke
subtitle: 64 y/o female, witnessed sudden right-sided deficits, in the alteplase window
nursesNote: 0712 — 64 y/o female brought in by EMS after sudden onset of right-sided facial droop, right arm weakness, and slurred speech witnessed by spouse at 0635. Last known well 0630. NIHSS at triage: 14. Spouse denies recent surgery, head trauma, GI bleeding, or anticoagulant use. CT head without contrast at 0745 — no hemorrhage, no early ischemic changes. Neurology at bedside; client is being evaluated for IV alteplase pending BP control and final inclusion-criteria review.

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL clinically relevant cues for this client.

opts.text: Sudden right-sided facial droop and arm weakness witnessed at 0635
opts.rat: Acute focal neurologic deficits with abrupt onset are the cardinal sign of stroke (FAST/BE-FAST).

opts.text: Slurred speech (dysarthria)
opts.rat: Dysarthria/aphasia indicates cortical or brainstem ischemia and contributes to the NIHSS score.

opts.text: Last known well at 0630
opts.rat: The IV alteplase window is up to 4.5 hours from last known well — not from symptom discovery or ED arrival (AHA/ASA 2019).

opts.text: BP 204/112 mmHg
opts.rat: Severe hypertension is a thrombolytic exclusion until lowered to <185/110 to reduce intracerebral hemorrhage risk.

opts.text: NIHSS score 14
opts.rat: NIHSS quantifies stroke severity; a score of 14 is moderate-to-severe and supports thrombolytic benefit.

opts.text: CT head shows no hemorrhage and no early ischemic changes
opts.rat: A negative non-contrast CT excludes intracerebral hemorrhage, the mandatory prerequisite for alteplase.

opts.text: Glucose 142 mg/dL
opts.rat: Mildly elevated but well above the hypoglycemia threshold that would suggest a stroke mimic; not a thrombolytic exclusion.

opts.text: INR 1.0, platelets 245, aPTT 28
opts.rat: All coagulation parameters are normal — these support eligibility but are not abnormal cues.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select the statements that correctly link the cues to underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: Focal deficits result from arterial occlusion creating an ischemic core surrounded by a salvageable penumbra
opts.rat: The core is irreversibly infarcted within minutes; the penumbra remains viable for hours if reperfusion is achieved (Lewis Med-Surg 11e, p. 1395).

opts.text: Elevated BP is largely a compensatory response to maintain cerebral perfusion to the penumbra
opts.rat: Cerebral autoregulation is impaired in the penumbra, making perfusion pressure-dependent.

opts.text: Aggressive BP lowering before reperfusion can extend the infarct by reducing penumbral perfusion
opts.rat: Overshoot lowering can convert salvageable penumbra to infarcted tissue; titrate carefully (AHA/ASA 2019).

opts.text: The 4.5-hour alteplase window starts from the time of ED arrival
opts.rat: Incorrect — the window starts from LAST KNOWN WELL. Most commonly missed concept and a major safety issue.

opts.text: Alteplase reverses damage already completed in the infarct core
opts.rat: Alteplase only salvages the penumbra by lysing the clot; the core infarct is irreversible at presentation.

opts.text: A negative head CT definitively rules out acute ischemic stroke
opts.rat: Non-contrast CT is highly sensitive for hemorrhage but acute ischemia is often invisible for 6-24 hours.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: RANK the differential diagnoses from MOST likely (1) to LEAST likely (5).

opts.text: Acute ischemic stroke — large-vessel (likely left MCA) territory
opts.rat: Sudden contralateral focal deficits, NIHSS 14, witnessed onset, and CT excluding bleed make ischemic stroke overwhelmingly the most likely diagnosis.

opts.text: Hemorrhagic stroke (intracerebral hemorrhage)
opts.rat: Always in the differential for sudden focal deficit, but the negative non-contrast CT effectively excludes acute hemorrhage at this time.

opts.text: Stroke mimic — hypoglycemia
opts.rat: Always assessed early because it is rapidly reversible, but ruled out by glucose 142.

opts.text: Stroke mimic — complex migraine or Todd's paralysis
opts.rat: Possible but no headache aura history, no witnessed seizure activity, and deficits are persistent.

opts.text: Conversion (functional) disorder
opts.rat: Least likely: deficits follow a clear vascular distribution and are objectively demonstrable. Diagnosis of exclusion.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Classify each intervention as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED at this time.

opts.text: Administer IV labetalol per stroke protocol to lower BP to <185/110 prior to alteplase
opts.rat: BP must be <185/110 before alteplase and <180/105 for 24 hours after, to minimize symptomatic ICH risk (AHA/ASA 2019).

opts.text: Administer alteplase 0.9 mg/kg IV (10% bolus, 90% infusion over 60 min) once BP and inclusion criteria are met
opts.rat: Standard adult dose for AIS within 4.5 hours of last known well; max total dose 90 mg.

opts.text: Administer aspirin 325 mg PO immediately
opts.rat: Antiplatelets must be HELD for 24 hours after alteplase to avoid hemorrhagic conversion.

opts.text: Initiate continuous cardiac monitoring with neuro checks every 15 minutes
opts.rat: q15-min neuro checks and BP monitoring are standard during alteplase infusion and for 2 hours post-infusion to detect ICH or angioedema early.

opts.text: Bolus 1 L of 0.9% normal saline wide open
opts.rat: No evidence of hypovolemia; a wide-open bolus would worsen hypertension and could increase cerebral edema.

opts.text: Keep client NPO until a bedside swallow screen is performed
opts.rat: AHA/ASA mandates a validated dysphagia screen before any oral intake (including PO meds) to prevent aspiration pneumonia.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5) you would implement.

opts.text: Place client on continuous cardiac/BP monitoring and initiate q15-min neuro checks
opts.rat: FIRST — establishes the safety baseline before any intervention.

opts.text: Administer IV labetalol per protocol to bring BP <185/110
opts.rat: SECOND — alteplase is contraindicated above this threshold; titrate carefully to avoid overshoot.

opts.text: Confirm all alteplase inclusion/exclusion criteria with the neurologist and obtain informed consent
opts.rat: THIRD — final eligibility check immediately before the bolus.

opts.text: Administer alteplase 0.9 mg/kg IV (10% bolus, then 90% infusion over 60 minutes)
opts.rat: FOURTH — door-to-needle goal ≤45 minutes per AHA/ASA.

opts.text: Transfer to the Neuro ICU for ongoing post-thrombolysis monitoring
opts.rat: LAST — ICU-level monitoring is required for at least the first 24 hours.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that indicate a POSITIVE response to the interventions.

opts.text: NIHSS improved from 14 to 6 at 24 hours post-alteplase
opts.rat: A reduction of ≥4 points on the NIHSS is clinically meaningful and reflects successful reperfusion of the penumbra.

opts.text: BP controlled at 162/88 mmHg throughout the alteplase infusion
opts.rat: Maintaining BP <180/105 during and 24 hours after alteplase is the AHA/ASA target.

opts.text: Client passes bedside dysphagia screen and tolerates small sips of water without coughing
opts.rat: Permits safe initiation of oral intake and PO medications; signals adequate bulbar function.

opts.text: Sudden severe headache, nausea, and vomiting 4 hours after alteplase infusion
opts.rat: NOT positive — classic presentation of symptomatic ICH post-thrombolysis. STOP infusion, stat CT, notify provider.

opts.text: Right-sided facial droop resolved; only mild residual right hand weakness remains
opts.rat: Resolution of major focal deficits reflects successful reperfusion and salvage of the penumbra.

opts.text: Tongue and lip swelling noted 30 minutes into the alteplase infusion
opts.rat: NOT positive — orolingual angioedema, an alteplase reaction (1-5%, higher on ACE inhibitors). Stop infusion and protect airway.

opts.text: No bleeding from IV sites, gums, or urinary catheter; no new bruising
opts.rat: Absence of systemic bleeding is a positive safety finding given alteplase's systemic fibrinolytic effect.

opts.text: NIHSS unchanged at 14 with no new deficits at 24 hours
opts.rat: NOT positive — this is stability, not improvement. Lack of NIHSS reduction suggests failed reperfusion; consider thrombectomy evaluation.
